{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/events","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11","id":1733573945,"node_id":"I_kwDOJlEtCs5nVD05","number":11,"title":"Discussion about the storage of headers","user":{"login":"rajkumardongre","id":77038993,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc3MDM4OTkz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/77038993?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre","html_url":"https://github.com/rajkumardongre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-05-31T07:38:15Z","updated_at":"2023-06-01T15:34:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, I am considering having two properties in our `response_type` for storing **headers**. The first property, `header_string`, would store headers as a plain string without any processing(as it is from the [fortran-curl](https://github.com/interkosmos/fortran-curl)). The second property, `header`, would store headers in a **key-value pair** inside a hashmap using the [fhash](https://github.com/LKedward/fhash) library. With these two properties, users could access headers in two ways: 
```fortran
response%header_string
``` 
and 
```fortran
response%header
```
I have added an example of this in my recent [commit](https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/pull/10/commits/90f0a9539e90379e16e69133b420c34c64bdb06d) for further insight. 

I would like to hear your thoughts and suggestions on this approach.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/comments/1572283627","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11#issuecomment-1572283627","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11","id":1572283627,"node_id":"IC_kwDOJlEtCs5dtyTr","user":{"login":"milancurcic","id":4133310,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQxMzMzMTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4133310?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic","html_url":"https://github.com/milancurcic","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-06-01T15:34:50Z","updated_at":"2023-06-01T15:34:50Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I suggest making `header_string` and `header` private and hiding them from the user (by making them `private`).

Rationale:

1. Having to parse `header_string` yourself is not the nicest UX; I think we can do better.
2. Iterating over the hashmap is not the nicest UX either because the user would need to learn the hashmap API. I think hashmap should be an internal dependency and hidden from the user.

Instead, consider making a small API as part of the `response_type` to get all header keys, as well as to get the value of a specific header. For example:

```
response%header_keys()  ! returns an array of strings
```

There are options for how to return an array of strings. It could be an array of `character` variables that have the length of the longest header key, and the shorter ones are space padded. It could also by an array of `string_type` variables from stdlib.

To get the specific header value, we'd have a function:

```
response%header_value(key)  ! accepts a string and returns a string
```

Although some header values can be non-strings (typically integer numbers), here I suggest simply returning a `character` string as the result (even for numbers, e.g. "1234" instead of 1234) so that we don't force polymorphic type guarding on the user. Instead, at least to start, the user always gets the string and they can convert it to numerical values if they need that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/comments/1572283627/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/pulls/comments/1213345179","pull_request_review_id":1455778355,"id":1213345179,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOJlEtCs5IUi2b","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,63 @@
+program test_get
+    use iso_fortran_env, only: stderr => error_unit
+    use fhash, only:  key => fhash_key, fhash_iter_t, fhash_key_t
+    use http, only : response_type, request
+    implicit none

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11/events","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11","id":1733573945,"node_id":"I_kwDOJlEtCs5nVD05","number":11,"title":"Discussion about the storage of headers","user":{"login":"rajkumardongre","id":77038993,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc3MDM4OTkz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/77038993?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre","html_url":"https://github.com/rajkumardongre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-05-31T07:38:15Z","updated_at":"2023-06-02T18:51:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, I am considering having two properties in our `response_type` for storing **headers**. The first property, `header_string`, would store headers as a plain string without any processing(as it is from the [fortran-curl](https://github.com/interkosmos/fortran-curl)). The second property, `header`, would store headers in a **key-value pair** inside a hashmap using the [fhash](https://github.com/LKedward/fhash) library. With these two properties, users could access headers in two ways: 
```fortran
response%header_string
``` 
and 
```fortran
response%header
```
I have added an example of this in my recent [commit](https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/pull/10/commits/90f0a9539e90379e16e69133b420c34c64bdb06d) for further insight.

Making Header access more [user friendly](https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/11#issuecomment-1572283627) and [Performing all cURL related tasks in a single function or subroutine](https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/20#issuecomment-1575581808)
* Rename the `response_type` `header` to `header_fhash` and make it private.
* Remove the `header_string`, find it redundant
* Add stdlib as a dependency
* Added derived type `header_type`
* Remove curl_ptr and header_list_ptr from derived types.
* Add installation of libcurl in linux
* Update the tests and example files accordingly. * Rename the `response_type` `header` to `header_fhash` and make it private.
* Remove the `header_string`, find it redundant
* Add stdlib as a dependency
* Added derived type `header_type`
* Remove curl_ptr and header_list_ptr from derived types.
* Add installation of libcurl in linux
* Update the tests and example files accordingly. Cookies","user":{"login":"rajkumardongre","id":77038993,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc3MDM4OTkz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/77038993?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre","html_url":"https://github.com/rajkumardongre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-06-07T11:01:10Z","updated_at":"2023-06-07T16:24:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"To store response cookies, I am considering creating a property called `cookies` inside `http_response` which will be of type `cookie_store_type`. Here is the definition of `cookie_store_type`:
```fortran
 type :: cookie_store_type
    type(fhash_tbl_t), private :: cookies
    type(string_type), private, allocatable :: cookie_names(:)
    integer :: cookie_count = 0	 
 end type cookie_store_type
```
The `cookies` property will maintain a hash table with the **cookie name** as a key of type string. Corresponding to that key, it will have a **`cookie_type` object** that stores all the information about a **single cookie**. Here is the definition of `cookie_type`:
```fortran
type :: cookie_type
    character(:), allocatable :: name, value, path, expires, domain 
    logical :: http_only, same_site, secure
 end type cookie_type
```
Since we can get cookies inside our **response headers**, specifically in the `Set-Cookie` header, I plan to extract the cookies from the `Set-Cookie` header and then parse the cookies to populate the `cookie_type` object. 
Finally, I will store this `cookie_type` object in `response%cookies%cookies` (of type hash map), having the **cookie name** as the key and the `cookie_type` object as the value.

So top level API to access response cookie will be :
```fortran
type(cookie_type) :: cookie
cookie = response%cookies%value('cookie_name')
print *, cookie%name
print *, cookie%value
print *, cookie% path
print *, cookie%expires
print *, cookie%, domain 
```
here `response%cookies%value('cookie_name')` is use to **access a single cookie**.
For **getting all cookies** we can use this : 
```fortran
type(string_type), allocatable :: cookie_keys
type(cookie_type) :: cookie
integer :: i
cookie_list = response%cookies%keys()
do i = 1, size(cookie_list)
   cookie = response%cookies%value(cookie_list(i))
   print *, cookie%name
   print *, cookie%value
   print *, cookie% path
   print *, cookie%expires
   print *, cookie%, domain 
end do
```
I would like to hear your thoughts and suggestions on this approach.

I have come up with an idea for passing headers to an HTTP request. My idea involves creating a derived type named `header_type`, below is it's defination : 
```fortran
type :: header_type
    type(c_ptr) :: header_list_ptr = c_null_ptr
    integer :: header_count = 0
contains
    procedure :: set_header
end type header_type
```
since curl maintains a linked list for storing the request header, `header_list_ptr` will point to the head of the linked list, `header_count` will maintain the total number of headers passed to the request. `set_header` subroutine will take the header `key` and `value` as input argument and store it inside the header linked list in the required format.

Here is an example of how this approach can be used to add headers to an HTTP request:
```fortran
  use http, only: response_type, request, header_type
  implicit none
  type(response_type) :: response
  type(header_type) :: request_header
  
  ! Setting request header
  call request_header%set_header('h1', 'v1')
  call request_header%set_header('h2', 'v2')
  call request_header%set_header('h3', 'v3')

  response = request(url='https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos/1', header=request_header)
```
I have added an example of this approach in my recent [commit](https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/pull/19/commits/9c5bfce83ad87775bba825e659cd51b1843e92e7) for further insight. I would appreciate your thoughts and suggestions on this approach.

I have come up with an idea for passing headers to an HTTP request. My idea involves creating a derived type named `header_type`, below is it's defination : 
```fortran
type :: header_type
    type(c_ptr) :: header_list_ptr = c_null_ptr
    integer :: header_count = 0
contains
    procedure :: set_header
end type header_type
```
since curl maintains a linked list for storing the request header, `header_list_ptr` will point to the head of the linked list, `header_count` will maintain the total number of headers passed to the request. `set_header` subroutine will take the header `key` and `value` as input argument and store it inside the header linked list in the required format.

Here is an example of how this approach can be used to add headers to an HTTP request:
```fortran
  use http, only: response_type, request, header_type
  implicit none
  type(response_type) :: response
  type(header_type) :: request_header
  
  ! Setting request header
  call request_header%set_header('h1', 'v1')
  call request_header%set_header('h2', 'v2')
  call request_header%set_header('h3', 'v3')

  response = request(url='https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos/1', header=request_header)
```
I have added an example of this approach in my recent [commit](https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/pull/19/commits/9c5bfce83ad87775bba825e659cd51b1843e92e7) for further insight. I would appreciate your thoughts and suggestions on this approach.

Cookies","user":{"login":"rajkumardongre","id":77038993,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc3MDM4OTkz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/77038993?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre","html_url":"https://github.com/rajkumardongre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-06-07T11:01:10Z","updated_at":"2023-06-07T19:04:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"To store response cookies, I am considering creating a property called `cookies` inside `http_response` which will be of type `cookie_store_type`. Here is the definition of `cookie_store_type`:
```fortran
 type :: cookie_store_type
    type(fhash_tbl_t), private :: cookies
    type(string_type), private, allocatable :: cookie_names(:)
    integer :: cookie_count = 0	 
 end type cookie_store_type
```
The `cookies` property will maintain a hash table with the **cookie name** as a key of type string. Corresponding to that key, it will have a **`cookie_type` object** that stores all the information about a **single cookie**. Here is the definition of `cookie_type`:
```fortran
type :: cookie_type
    character(:), allocatable :: name, value, path, expires, domain 
    logical :: http_only, same_site, secure
 end type cookie_type
```
Since we can get cookies inside our **response headers**, specifically in the `Set-Cookie` header, I plan to extract the cookies from the `Set-Cookie` header and then parse the cookies to populate the `cookie_type` object. 
Finally, I will store this `cookie_type` object in `response%cookies%cookies` (of type hash map), having the **cookie name** as the key and the `cookie_type` object as the value.

So top level API to access response cookie will be :
```fortran
type(cookie_type) :: cookie
cookie = response%cookies%value('cookie_name')
print *, cookie%name
print *, cookie%value
print *, cookie% path
print *, cookie%expires
print *, cookie%, domain 
```
here `response%cookies%value('cookie_name')` is use to **access a single cookie**.
For **getting all cookies** we can use this : 
```fortran
type(string_type), allocatable :: cookie_keys
type(cookie_type) :: cookie
integer :: i
cookie_list = response%cookies%keys()
do i = 1, size(cookie_list)
   cookie = response%cookies%value(cookie_list(i))
   print *, cookie%name
   print *, cookie%value
   print *, cookie% path
   print *, cookie%expires
   print *, cookie%, domain 
end do
```
I would like to hear your thoughts and suggestions on this approach.

* Replace hashmap with header_type array to store request and response headers

* Remove unused import

* Explicit import

* Use optval for optional args default values

* remove set_default_headers

* migrate append_header procedure to http_response.f90

* Using move_alloc() to append response header

* Explicit imports

* Remove no longer used append_header

---------

The first property, `header_string`, would store headers as a plain string without any processing(as it is from the [fortran-curl](https://github.com/interkosmos/fortran-curl)). The second property, `header`, would store headers in a **key-value pair** inside a hashmap using the [fhash](https://github.com/LKedward/fhash) library. With these two properties, users could access headers in two ways: 
```fortran
response%header_string
``` 
and 
```fortran
response%header
```

How do you think we should expand the `request` API to allow for:

1. URL encoded form data via `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` content type
2. Binary data via `multipart/form-data` content type

Python `requests` distinguishes this based on the type of input to the `data` argument. I.e. if `data` is a `dict`, it's a URL form encoded (IIRC). If it's a `bytes` or `file` object, it goes as `multipart/form-data`.  

We could take a similar approach, i.e. control the request content type based on the type of the `data` input argument.

While this is all most useful with `POST` requests, it applies to all other request methods, e.g. it's possible to send data in the body of a `GET` request, although normally servers should ignore such data.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/comments/1593384188/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

s":[{"login":"rajkumardongre","id":77038993,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc3MDM4OTkz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/77038993?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre","html_url":"https://github.com/rajkumardongre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rajkumardongre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-06-19T20:53:15Z","updated_at":"2023-06-19T20:53:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/http-client/pull/27 introduced a new API to the `request` function which now accepts an optional `json` argument. This is generally fine except that on our Friday call we agreed that rather than `json` we'd call this argument `data` as it can accept any kind of data. The only thing specific here is that this sets `Content-Type` to `application/json`, but the user can set that via `headers`.

On our Friday call, we agreed that we'd call this argument `data`, and what you have currently proposed as `data` in #24, we would call `form`.

Continuing from our call, and what should have been noted in #24, let's proceed with the following API:

```fortran
function new_request(url, method, header, data) result(response)
    integer, intent(in), optional :: method
    character(len=*), intent(in) :: url
    character(len=*), intent(in), optional :: data
    type(header_type), intent(in), optional :: header(:)
```

Let's tackle this first before any other changes to the API. For the time being, the user will be responsible for setting the appropriate `Content-Type`, whether that's `application/json` or otherwise.

We can then discuss any possible API for setting type data.

I have simplified the headers API. Rather than introducing a new procedure that does two things (check for presence of key and call `append_header`), I have removed `put_if_header_absent` and introduced `header_has_key` function which simply checks for existence of a key. This makes the API more horizontal in the sense that independent functionality is orthogonal and each procedure does only one thing.

I have also introduced a test suite for headers. Going forward, for any new functionality and module, please also add a test suite program. I have also added and edited existing docstrings in http_header.f90 into FORD-style docstrings. This will allow us easy generation of browsable API docs. Going forward, please use the same dosctring style when documenting functions.

I'm still not 100% happy with how we're setting default headers in the `new_request` function. Currently we're duplicating the default header keys and values. I'll think about how better to approach this but for now it's have done some changes in `set_body` function to send files also : \r\n\r\nThe `set_body` function determines the type of data to include in the request body  based on the inputs provided. If `data` is provided, it is sent as the body of the  request. If `form` is provided without a `file`, the form data is URL encoded and sent as the body of the request. If `file` is provided without `form`, the `file` is sent using a multipart/form-data header. If both `form` and `file` are provided, the `file` takes priority and the `form` along with `file` is sent as part of the multipart/form-data body. If `data`, `form`, and `file` are all provided, only `data` is sent and the `form` and `file` inputs are In other words, do we need a call to `request % send` that is separate from the request constructor?

If not, why not design a more functional constructor approach where a request function returns a response type, like in Python requests?

```fortran
use http_client, only: http_request, http_response_type, HTTP_GET

type(http_response_type) :: response

response = http_request("http://example.com")
! response = http_request("http://example.com", method=HTTP_GET) ! Same thing as above

print *, response % status_code ! 200 if ok
print *, response % headers ! array of key-val pairs
print *, response % body ! character string
```

I suggest adopting a small set of principles to stick to, and only relax them when necessary. These could be:

* All information from the user is passed to type constructor functions
* Return type instances are read-only
* No setters or getters

In summary, start small and simple, and only add complexity when needed.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/http-client/issues/comments/1557281980/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+  use, intrinsic :: iso_fortran_env, only: i4 => int32, i8 => int64, r4 => real32, r8 => real64
+  use curl
   implicit none
   private
+  ! HTTP methods:
+  integer, parameter, public :: HTTP_GET    = 1
+  integer, parameter, public :: HTTP_HEAD   = 2
+  integer, parameter, public :: HTTP_POST   = 3
+  integer, parameter, public :: HTTP_PUT    = 4
+  integer, parameter, public :: HTTP_DELETE = 5
+  integer, parameter, public :: HTTP_PATCH  = 6
 
-  public :: say_hello
+  ! Request Type
+  type :: request_type
+    character(len=:), allocatable :: url
+    character(len=:), allocatable :: method
+  end type request_type
+
+  ! Response Type
+  type, public :: response_type
+    character(len=:), allocatable :: content
+    character(len=:), allocatable :: url
+    character(len=:), allocatable :: method
+    integer                       :: status_code
+    integer(kind=c_size_t)                      :: content_length = 0 
+  end type response_type
+  
+  ! http_client Type
+  type :: client_type
+    type(request_type) :: request
+  contains
+    procedure :: client_get_response 
+  end type client_type
+
+  interface client_type
+    module procedure new_client
+  end interface client_type
+
+  interface http_request
+    module procedure new_request
+  end interface http_request
+
+  ! Procedure defination
+  public :: http_request
+  
 contains
-  subroutine say_hello
-    print *, "Hello, http-client!"
-  end subroutine say_hello
+
+
+! Constructor for request_type type.
+function new_request(url, method) result(response)
+  character(len=*)                              :: url
+  integer, optional                             :: method
+  type(request_type)                            :: request
+  type(response_type)                           :: response
+  type(client_type)                             :: client
+
+  if(present(method)) then 
+    if(method == 1) then
+      request%method = 'GET'  
+    else if (method == 2) then
+      request%method = 'HEAD'  
+    else if (method == 3) then
+      request%method = 'POST'  
+    else if (method == 4) then
+      request%method = 'PUT'  
+    else if(method == 5) then
+      request%method = 'DELETE' 
+    else if( method == 6) then
+      request%method = 'PATCH'  
+    end if      
+  else
+    request%method = 'GET'
+  end if
+  request%url = url
+  client = client_type(request=request)
+  response = client%client_get_response()
+  
+end function new_request
+
+function client_get_response(this) result(response)
+  class(client_type)                            :: this
+  type(response_type), target                   :: response
+  type(c_ptr)                                   :: curl_ptr
+  integer                                       :: rc
+  ! logic for populating response using fortran-curl
+  response%url = this%request%url
+  response%method = this%request%method
+
+  curl_ptr = curl_easy_init()
+
+  if (.not. c_associated(curl_ptr)) then
+    stop 'Error: curl_easy_init() failed'
+  end if
+  ! setting request URL
+  rc = curl_easy_setopt(curl_ptr, CURLOPT_URL,              this%request%url // c_null_char)
+  ! setting request method
+  rc = curl_easy_setopt(curl_ptr, CURLOPT_CUSTOMREQUEST,    this%request%method // c_null_char)
+  ! setting callback for writing received data
+  rc = curl_easy_setopt(curl_ptr, CURLOPT_WRITEFUNCTION,    c_funloc(client_response_callback))
+  ! setting response pointer to write callback
+  rc = curl_easy_setopt(curl_ptr, CURLOPT_WRITEDATA, {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/863","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/863/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/863/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/863/events","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/863","id":1650756122,"node_id":"I_kwDODe7zws5iZIoa","number":863,"title":"fpm doesn't recognize .f extension","user":{"login":"rouson","id":13108868,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzMTA4ODY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13108868?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson","html_url":"https://github.com/rouson","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rouson/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1786237147,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzg2MjM3MTQ3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"d73a4a","default":true,"description":"Something isn't working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-04-02T00:52:27Z","updated_at":"2023-04-02T05:16:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

There seem to be at least 5 issues that have discussed fixed source form and/or the `.f` extension: #250, #359, #363, #366, #367.  I don't have a strong opinion about the best path forward, but I suspect that any of the previously suggested options would be preferable to the status quo.  From the test transcript below, I gather that `fpm` currently doesn't recognize files with the `.f` extension as Fortran source files.  However, if a file has the extension `.f90` and contains fixed-form source, then `fpm build --flag "-ffixed-form"` succeeds (with `gfortran`). This has the counter-intuitive effect that a file containing fixed-form source must have a name with an extension that is the _opposite_ of `gfortran`' s default convention in order for `fpm build` to work.  Is there a decision-making process that can be used to decide which of the many suggested paths forward to choose?  Even if the chosen solution doesn't get implemented right away, at least making a decision could facilitate closing some of the aforementioned issues so that people searching the open issues will find only the chosen path rather than all of the alternatives considered.

```
% tree .
.
├── app
│   └── main.f
└── fpm.toml

1 directory, 2 files

% cat fpm.toml 
name = "hello"
version = "0.1.0"
author = "Damian Rouson"
maintainer = "damian@archaeologic.codes"

% cat app/main.f 
      program main
        implicit none
        print *,"Hello, world!" 
      end program main

% fpm build --flag "-ffixed-form" 
<ERROR>*cmd_build*:package error:Neither library nor executable found, there is nothing to do
STOP 1

% mv app/main.f{,90}

% fpm build --flag "-ffixed-form"
 + mkdir -p build/dependencies
main.f90                               done.
hello                                  done.
[100%] Project compiled successfully.
```

### Expected Behaviour

I would expect files with the `.f` extension to at least be recognized as Fortran source files independent of whether the default is to treat them as fixed or free source form.

### Version of fpm

0.7.0

### Platform and Architecture

macOS/ARM

has been hanging around stale long enough, here is a draft implementation for disabling implicit typing and external interfaces by default with opt-in toggles on per package basis. Also allows to switch globally between fixed and free format for source files.

- [x] *implicit-typing*: toggle default implicit typing rules
  - option to disable in GFortran
  - option to enable in LFortran
- [x] *implicit-external*: toggle implicit external interfaces
  - option to disable in GFortran
  - option to enable in LFortran
- [x] *source-format*: select source format ("free", "fixed", or "default")
  -  option to set free/fixed format in GFortran
  - option to set fixed format in LFortran

*Usage*

```toml
[fortran]
implicit-typing = true  # default: false
implicit-external = true  # default: false
source-format = "fixed"  # default: "free"
```

Documentation at https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm-docs/pull/98
Closes #577 
Closes #363

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/webpage/pulls/comments/1122186194","pull_request_review_id":1320453029,"id":1122186194,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOHubBe85C4zPS","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,81 @@
+# Fortran-lang history
+
+## Creation
+
+The Fortran-lang project was started at the beginning of 2020 by Ondřej Čertík
+and Milan Curcic
+([[1]](https://ondrejcertik.com/blog/2021/03/resurrecting-fortran/),
+[[2]](https://medium.com/modern-fortran/first-year-of-fortran-lang-d8796bfa0067)).
+The effort built upon work of several members of the Fortran Standards
+Committee that built a GitHub proposals repository to track proposals to the
+Standards Committee, which lead to the creation of the Fortran Standard Library
+(`stdlib`), the Fortran Package Manager (`fpm`) and the `fortran-lang.org`
+website. The webpage quickly become the top result in major search engines for
+"Fortran". We created a Fortran Discourse where the whole Fortran community can
+meet and discuss, make announcements, ask for help, etc. The Fortran-lang
+effort was joined by hundreds of contributors. At the end of 2022 the
+Fortran-lang project was accepted under NumFOCUS for fiscal sponsorship.
+
+## Incubator J3 repo
+
+## The Fortran Standard Library `stdlib`
+
+Created in the late 2019 the Fortran Standard Library provides
+a community driven and agreed upon de facto "standard" library for Fortran.
+The library is a collection of modules that provide a wide range of
+functionality, including: containers, sorting, searching, linear algebra,
+unities, fast Fourier transforms, and more.
+
+Find out more about `stdlib` on our [website](https://stdlib.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib).
+
+## The Fortran Package Manager `fpm`
+
+## Fortran Discourse
+
+## Fortran Package Index
+
+## Project migration to Fortran-lang
+
+Over time crucial projects to the Fortran community were migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization to allow for community collaboration and to ensure
+long-term sustainability of the projects.
+
+### fftpack
+
+### minipack
+
+### test-drive
+
+### vscode-fortran-support
+
+<!-- Migration date: 22-04-2022 -->
+
+Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code is feature-rich Visual extension
+for Fortran originally created by
+Miguel Carvajal and maintained by Giannis Nikiteas.
+The extension migrated in early 2022 to the Fortran-lang organization
+to improve integration with other Fortran-lang projects such as `fpm`
+and allow for easier community collaboration.
+
+Find out more about Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code on
+[VS Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=fortran-lang.linter-gfortran)
+and on [GitHub](httos://github.com/fortran-lang/vscode-fortran-support).
+
+### fortls
+
+<!-- Migration date: 28-11-2022 -->
+
+`fortls` is a Fortran language server created by Giannis Nikiteas
+in late 2019. The project started as a fork of the
+archived project `fortran-language-server` but quickly diverged from it
+both in terms of features and codebase. The project migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization in late 2022 to allow for easier community
+collaboration.
+
+Find out more about `fortls` on our [website](https://fortls.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/fortls).
+
+## Fortran Publication
+

```suggestion
# Fortran-lang History

* **October 2019**: J3 members created the [j3-fortran/fortran_proposals](https://github.com/j3-fortran/fortran_proposals) to bridge the gap between J3 and the broader Fortran community. Many people, J3 members and non-members alike, contributed proposals, requests for improvement, and discussions.
* **December 2019**: We created the incubator repository for the Fortran Standard Library ([fortran-lang/stdlib](https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib)), and the [Fortran-lang GitHub organization](https://github.com/fortran-lang) to host the Fortran stdlib repository. We also created the [Fortran-lang Twitter account](https://twitter.com/fortranlang) and the incubator repository for the [Fortran Package Manager (fpm)](https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm) and its first minimal prototype in Rust.
* **February 2020**: We began work on the next implementation of the fpm prototype in Haskell.
* **April 2020**: We created the [Fortran-lang website](https://fortran-lang.org) and presented efforts on the [Open Source Directions podcast](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2NiS2tdDO_4).
* **May 2020**: We created the [Fortran Discourse](https://fortran-lang.discourse.group/).
* **July 2020**: We presented our efforts at the first international Fortran conference ([FortranCon 2020](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLeKbr7eYHjt77h90hDVC-vGzrWmvDhCAf). We also began work on the Fortran implementation of fpm.
* **November 2020**: We began work on the Fortran implementation of fpm.
* **February 2021**: Fortran-lang accepted into the Google Summer of Code 2021 program.
* **March 2021**: [test-drive](https://github.com/fortran-lang/test-drive) framework migrated and maintained at Fortran-lang.
* **July 2021**: Modernized [fftpack](https://github.com/fortran-lang/fftpack) and [minpack](https://github.com/fortran-lang/minpack) packages migrated and maintained at Fortran-lang.
* **September 2021**: We presented our efforts at [FortranCon 2021](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=g7ECnr_Q7Ck&list=PLeKbr7eYHjt5UaV9zQtY24oEbne9_uFni). We also published the [Toward Modern Fortran Tooling and a Thriving Developer
Community](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2109.07382.pdf) paper on arXiv.
* **November 2021**: We presented fpm at the [PackagingCon 2021](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YG8zEM1lAVM).
* **February 2022**: Fortran-lang accepted into the Google Summer of Code 2022 program.
* **March 2022**: [The State of Fortran](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/stamp/stamp.jsp?arnumber=9736688) paper published in IEEE CiSE.
* **April 2022**: Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code ([VS Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=fortran-lang.linter-gfortran), 
[GitHub](httos://github.com/fortran-lang/vscode-fortran-support)) joined Fortran-lang.
* **November 2022**: Fortran-lang applied to become a member project of NumFOCUS. [Fortran Language Server (fortls)](https://fortls.fortran-lang.org/) joined Fortran-lang.
* **February 2023**: Fortran-lang accepted into the Google Summer of Code 2023 program.

+# Fortran-lang history
+
+## Creation
+
+The Fortran-lang project was started at the beginning of 2020 by Ondřej Čertík
+and Milan Curcic
+([[1]](https://ondrejcertik.com/blog/2021/03/resurrecting-fortran/),
+[[2]](https://medium.com/modern-fortran/first-year-of-fortran-lang-d8796bfa0067)).
+The effort built upon work of several members of the Fortran Standards
+Committee that built a GitHub proposals repository to track proposals to the
+Standards Committee, which lead to the creation of the Fortran Standard Library
+(`stdlib`), the Fortran Package Manager (`fpm`) and the `fortran-lang.org`
+website. The webpage quickly become the top result in major search engines for
+"Fortran". We created a Fortran Discourse where the whole Fortran community can
+meet and discuss, make announcements, ask for help, etc. The Fortran-lang
+effort was joined by hundreds of contributors. At the end of 2022 the
+Fortran-lang project was accepted under NumFOCUS for fiscal sponsorship.
+
+## Incubator J3 repo
+
+## The Fortran Standard Library `stdlib`
+
+Created in the late 2019 the Fortran Standard Library provides
+a community driven and agreed upon de facto "standard" library for Fortran.
+The library is a collection of modules that provide a wide range of
+functionality, including: containers, sorting, searching, linear algebra,
+unities, fast Fourier transforms, and more.
+
+Find out more about `stdlib` on our [website](https://stdlib.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib).
+
+## The Fortran Package Manager `fpm`
+
+## Fortran Discourse
+
+## Fortran Package Index
+
+## Project migration to Fortran-lang
+
+Over time crucial projects to the Fortran community were migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization to allow for community collaboration and to ensure
+long-term sustainability of the projects.
+
+### fftpack
+
+### minipack
+
+### test-drive
+
+### vscode-fortran-support
+
+<!-- Migration date: 22-04-2022 -->
+
+Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code is feature-rich Visual extension
+for Fortran originally created by
+Miguel Carvajal and maintained by Giannis Nikiteas.
+The extension migrated in early 2022 to the Fortran-lang organization
+to improve integration with other Fortran-lang projects such as `fpm`
+and allow for easier community collaboration.
+
+Find out more about Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code on
+[VS Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=fortran-lang.linter-gfortran)
+and on [GitHub](httos://github.com/fortran-lang/vscode-fortran-support).
+
+### fortls
+
+<!-- Migration date: 28-11-2022 -->
+
+`fortls` is a Fortran language server created by Giannis Nikiteas
+in late 2019. The project started as a fork of the
+archived project `fortran-language-server` but quickly diverged from it
+both in terms of features and codebase. The project migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization in late 2022 to allow for easier community
+collaboration.
+
+Find out more about `fortls` on our [website](https://fortls.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/fortls).
+

+# Fortran-lang history
+
+## Creation
+
+The Fortran-lang project was started at the beginning of 2020 by Ondřej Čertík
+and Milan Curcic
+([[1]](https://ondrejcertik.com/blog/2021/03/resurrecting-fortran/),
+[[2]](https://medium.com/modern-fortran/first-year-of-fortran-lang-d8796bfa0067)).
+The effort built upon work of several members of the Fortran Standards
+Committee that built a GitHub proposals repository to track proposals to the
+Standards Committee, which lead to the creation of the Fortran Standard Library
+(`stdlib`), the Fortran Package Manager (`fpm`) and the `fortran-lang.org`
+website. The webpage quickly become the top result in major search engines for
+"Fortran". We created a Fortran Discourse where the whole Fortran community can
+meet and discuss, make announcements, ask for help, etc. The Fortran-lang
+effort was joined by hundreds of contributors. At the end of 2022 the
+Fortran-lang project was accepted under NumFOCUS for fiscal sponsorship.
+
+## Incubator J3 repo
+
+## The Fortran Standard Library `stdlib`
+
+Created in the late 2019 the Fortran Standard Library provides
+a community driven and agreed upon de facto "standard" library for Fortran.
+The library is a collection of modules that provide a wide range of
+functionality, including: containers, sorting, searching, linear algebra,
+unities, fast Fourier transforms, and more.
+
+Find out more about `stdlib` on our [website](https://stdlib.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib).
+
+## The Fortran Package Manager `fpm`
+
+## Fortran Discourse
+
+## Fortran Package Index
+
+## Project migration to Fortran-lang
+
+Over time crucial projects to the Fortran community were migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization to allow for community collaboration and to ensure
+long-term sustainability of the projects.
+
+### fftpack
+
+### minipack
+
+### test-drive
+
+### vscode-fortran-support
+
+<!-- Migration date: 22-04-2022 -->
+
+Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code is feature-rich Visual extension
+for Fortran originally created by
+Miguel Carvajal and maintained by Giannis Nikiteas.
+The extension migrated in early 2022 to the Fortran-lang organization
+to improve integration with other Fortran-lang projects such as `fpm`
+and allow for easier community collaboration.
+
+Find out more about Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code on
+[VS Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=fortran-lang.linter-gfortran)
+and on [GitHub](httos://github.com/fortran-lang/vscode-fortran-support).
+
+### fortls
+
+<!-- Migration date: 28-11-2022 -->
+
+`fortls` is a Fortran language server created by Giannis Nikiteas
+in late 2019. The project started as a fork of the
+archived project `fortran-language-server` but quickly diverged from it
+both in terms of features and codebase. The project migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization in late 2022 to allow for easier community
+collaboration.
+
+Find out more about `fortls` on our [website](https://fortls.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/fortls).
+

+# Fortran-lang history
+
+## Creation
+
+The Fortran-lang project was started at the beginning of 2020 by Ondřej Čertík
+and Milan Curcic
+([[1]](https://ondrejcertik.com/blog/2021/03/resurrecting-fortran/),
+[[2]](https://medium.com/modern-fortran/first-year-of-fortran-lang-d8796bfa0067)).
+The effort built upon work of several members of the Fortran Standards
+Committee that built a GitHub proposals repository to track proposals to the
+Standards Committee, which lead to the creation of the Fortran Standard Library
+(`stdlib`), the Fortran Package Manager (`fpm`) and the `fortran-lang.org`
+website. The webpage quickly become the top result in major search engines for
+"Fortran". We created a Fortran Discourse where the whole Fortran community can
+meet and discuss, make announcements, ask for help, etc. The Fortran-lang
+effort was joined by hundreds of contributors. At the end of 2022 the
+Fortran-lang project was accepted under NumFOCUS for fiscal sponsorship.
+
+## Incubator J3 repo
+
+## The Fortran Standard Library `stdlib`
+
+Created in the late 2019 the Fortran Standard Library provides
+a community driven and agreed upon de facto "standard" library for Fortran.
+The library is a collection of modules that provide a wide range of
+functionality, including: containers, sorting, searching, linear algebra,
+unities, fast Fourier transforms, and more.
+
+Find out more about `stdlib` on our [website](https://stdlib.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib).
+
+## The Fortran Package Manager `fpm`
+
+## Fortran Discourse
+
+## Fortran Package Index
+
+## Project migration to Fortran-lang
+
+Over time crucial projects to the Fortran community were migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization to allow for community collaboration and to ensure
+long-term sustainability of the projects.
+
+### fftpack
+
+### minipack
+
+### test-drive
+
+### vscode-fortran-support
+
+<!-- Migration date: 22-04-2022 -->
+
+Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code is feature-rich Visual extension
+for Fortran originally created by
+Miguel Carvajal and maintained by Giannis Nikiteas.
+The extension migrated in early 2022 to the Fortran-lang organization
+to improve integration with other Fortran-lang projects such as `fpm`
+and allow for easier community collaboration.
+
+Find out more about Modern Fortran for Visual Studio Code on
+[VS Code Marketplace](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=fortran-lang.linter-gfortran)
+and on [GitHub](httos://github.com/fortran-lang/vscode-fortran-support).
+
+### fortls
+
+<!-- Migration date: 28-11-2022 -->
+
+`fortls` is a Fortran language server created by Giannis Nikiteas
+in late 2019. The project started as a fork of the
+archived project `fortran-language-server` but quickly diverged from it
+both in terms of features and codebase. The project migrated to the
+Fortran-lang organization in late 2022 to allow for easier community
+collaboration.
+
+Find out more about `fortls` on our [website](https://fortls.fortran-lang.org/)
+and on [GitHub](https://github.com/fortran-lang/fortls).
+
+## Fortran Publication
+

There seems to be a misunderstanding here. Your initial version only mentions you and I creating Fortran-lang, but doesn't give credit to creators of other projects. Format aside, I think this version was good, except that I only prefer our names not being explicitly mentioned. Then I understood your response as wanting to credit everybody who pushed each project. I think that's difficult to do and better to avoid here.

I don't think the question of format is the issue here. We just need to decide what to do with attribution. I don't think mentioning just you and me is a good solution because it omits many other people who contributed significantly more than either of us across the projects.

I think this doc, just like Fortran-lang, is about the community and not any single individual, and our writing should reflect that I didn't tell them much about the stdlib, instead I asked them to try it out by themselves. Most of them directly found the documentation, but they seemed to be a bit underwhelmed by it.

I'm not up-to-date with the progress of stdlib... sorry if I mention duplicate/obvious things.
Problems that occurred:
- missing or incomplete documentation
- not clear enough what *public* functions a module has (one student tried to use xoshiro256ss). I think it would be best to have a toggle for private functions
- navigating the docu: when searching in the documentation, it's easier to find the source file, which might be `.fypp` instead of the actual documentation which is a bit scary for students

After the initial frustration and confusion, they found many useful modules, e.g.:
- error_stop and check
- string type
- ascii
- bitsets
- io (npy)
- arange
- optval
- sorting
- stats

I think overall the routines convinced them, but the documentation definitely needs some improvement. That shouldn't be surprising.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/697/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/697/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/comments/1452183781","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/697#issuecomment-1452183781","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/697","id":1452183781,"node_id":"IC_kwDODZiDws5WjpDl","user":{"login":"milancurcic","id":4133310,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQxMzMzMTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4133310?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic","html_url":"https://github.com/milancurcic","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/milancurcic/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-02T16:44:53Z","updated_at":"2023-03-02T16:44:53Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Indeed. -02-22T12:55:12Z","updated_at":"2023-03-02T18:59:42Z","closed_at":"2023-03-02T18:59:42Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

See this job https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/actions/runs/4235861778/jobs/7360686209

and specifically the error message from the CMake compiler tester: https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/actions/runs/4235861778/jobs/7360686209#step:7:30

From the previous step, it looks like gfortran-9 is installed successfully via brew, however, I wonder if this link-time breakage is related to updates to the recent `macos-latest` change from v11 to v12 (https://github.com/actions/runner-images/issues/6384) I don't have any experience with macOS so it's not obvious to me what exactly causes the issue here.

Possible solutions:

* A macOS expert in the community can identify why gfortran-9 is not linking correctly and apply a fix;
* We remove gfortran-9 from the matrix for macOS.

To reduce complexity in the future, we could also remove the brew install step and only rely on GH Action supported compiler versions. On the current `macos-latest` (macOS 12), that's gfortran 11 and 12.

What do you think? I'm personally in favor of simply relying on the GitHub Actions provided compiler versions. For this specific issue, it would mean removing gfortran-9 from the matrix for macOS.

### Expected Behaviour

CI should succeed.

### Version of stdlib

fba31e0db6375e9207438d2815b8552dc87c6f0b

### Platform and Architecture

macos-latest (macos-12)

### Additional Information

documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-03-02T13:30:20Z","updated_at":"2023-03-02T19:37:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm currently giving a Fortran lecture with twelve students, and today I introduced modules, fpm dependencies and finally the stdlib.

I didn't tell them much about the stdlib, instead I asked them to try it out by themselves. Most of them directly found the documentation, but they seemed to be a bit underwhelmed by it.

I'm not up-to-date with the progress of stdlib... sorry if I mention duplicate/obvious things.
Problems that occurred:
- missing or incomplete documentation
- not clear enough what *public* functions a module has (one student tried to use xoshiro256ss). I think it would be best to have a toggle for private functions
- navigating the docu: when searching in the documentation, it's easier to find the source file, which might be `.fypp` instead of the actual documentation which is a bit scary for students

After the initial frustration and confusion, they found many useful modules, e.g.:
- error_stop and check
- string type
- ascii
- bitsets
- io (npy)
- arange
- optval
- sorting
- stats

I think overall the routines convinced them, but the documentation definitely needs some improvement. issues"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-09-13T16:08:31Z","updated_at":"2023-03-02T22:52:48Z","closed_at":"2023-03-02T22:52:47Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The container currently runs code as a root user which provides possibility to break the running instance of the container, at least until the Python server process is restarted. Let's create a non-root user in the Docker image and use that to run code. Perhaps something like this:

```Dockerfile
ENV USERNAME=fortran
RUN useradd \
  --no-log-init \
  --home-dir /home/$USERNAME \
  --create-home \
  --shell /bin/bash \
  $USERNAME
USER $USERNAME
WORKDIR /home/$USERNAME
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/playground/issues/46/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/playground/issues/46/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

In short, creative users can run programs that eat CPU for fun. The backend is running on a free VM that gives us a fraction of a CPU to work with, so it spends a lot of time throttled. That's why you get very slow responses or even a bad response from the server. Until it's properly secured, the backend relies on users playing nice. I agree it's not a good first experience. Yes, https://dev.lfortran.org is great. What exactly do you mean by invest in it? Of course, it doesn't need to be instead. I welcome and encourage people to contribute to both projects. Regarding the playground pointed to from play.fortran-lang.org, I think that's a good idea. The best playground should be pointed to from the website, and it's obvious to me that right now that's the LFortran playground. Why don't you open an issue in the webpage repo and gain support there, and we'll easily switch over? The key reason to have a backend is to allow running compilers that can't run in the browser (all of them except LFortran). The playground currently runs only GFortran, but it's a relatively straightforward to provide options to run other free compilers like ifort, ifx, flang, and lfortran. It's not a research problem but a matter of putting existing and well known pieces together. In case of LFortran, the playground could simply load it (as it's done on dev.lfortran.org) and run it in the browser instead of making HTTP requests to the backend. This could be a good advertisement for LFortran because all other compilers in the selection would take a significant fraction of a second (or even a few seconds) to upload the program, compile it, run it, and return the response, whereas LFortran would compile and run it instantly (assuming a sufficiently simple program). 2. Share the frontend and integrate complementary features. For example, dev.lfortran.org's examples gallery is very nice to use. The playground on the other hand has a tutorial capability. An integrated frontend would have both features, and of course, it would be one frontend codebase to maintain. 3. Share the examples and tutorial content--this is naturally part of the frontend, but could be set up as a static database of content (say, as a JSON and Fortran source files) that can be accessed from any frontend, even some new experimental frontend that somebody might be developing.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/playground/issues/comments/1454119419/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

a `zfill` function to left-pad a string with zeros. I have implemented it becuase I think it is a simple but useful function.

This would close issue #394.

It consists on an interface `zfill` pointing to functions `zfill_string` and `zfill_char` to use it with strings and chars respectively. The implementation is equivalent to the one in functions `padl_string_default` and `padl_char_default`.

I have prepared the documentation, examples and tests. Here's my opinion:

> o allow integer input arguments as well as strings?

I don't think so, we already have solid capability to go from an integer to string, so I think `zfill(string_type(123))` is a decent solution for this.

> o should the input string be cropped and then padded or should it retain original right-hand spaces?
> o does it handle signed values by moving the sign to the beginning of the string?

IMO, I don't think so, to keep the behavior simple, and I think the current description is appropriate in this regard (i.e. we describe what the function does, but not what it doesn't do).

> o should the length be optional, in which case it defaults to the length of input string when the input is a string?

I think that would be a good addition, let's evaluate it for a separate PR. I'll open an issue.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/comments/1454132595/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

strings","color":"62EF82","default":false,"description":"String processing"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2021-04-23T13:43:51Z","updated_at":"2023-03-03T21:28:15Z","closed_at":"2023-03-03T21:28:15Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Description**

A function to pad a string with zeros or another symbol to a given width could be helpful in many cases, especially together with the `to_string()` function for integer to string conversion.

Currently available methods to achieve this are using concatenation
```
width = 32
str = "hello"
padded_str = repeat('0',width-len(str)')//str
```
and perhaps also internal file I/O
```
write(buffer,'(A32)', blank='zero') "hello"   ! doesn't work for some reason
```

I propose to add the following functions
```
! left - padding
function padl(str,width,symbol) result(padded_str)
  character(len=*), intent(in) :: str
  integer, intent(in) :: width
  character(len=1), intent(in), optional :: symbol
  character(len=max(len(str),width) :: padded_str
end function
! right - padding
function padr(str,width,symbol) result(padded_str)
  character(len=*), intent(in) :: str
  integer, intent(in) :: width
  character(len=1), intent(in), optional :: symbol
  character(len=max(len(str),width) :: padded_str
end function
```

The original string is returned if `width < len(str)`. The default padding symbol could be either the blank symbol `' '` or `'0'`. Special treatment of a leading sign symbol `'+'`/`'-'` might be also desirable.

**Prior art**

* Python: [zfill](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html?highlight=zfill#str.zfill)
  (Another method to achieve padding in Python is using the string class method `.format()`)
* Julia: [lpad](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/strings/#Base.lpad), [rpad](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/strings/#Base.rpad)
* MATLAB: [pad](https://uk.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/pad.html)

{"repository_id":228099010,"push_id":12822002871,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/master","head":"8eaf156c499183002155b60b1933387a8169591e","before":"8f7ac8d3e2506d04b725d2315e9e7554389dec9e","commits":[{"sha":"8eaf156c499183002155b60b1933387a8169591e","author":{"email":"ff736d8e1d41b5230c57a05a1ca66056586559d2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"HugoMVale"},"message":"convert `pdf_norm` and `cdf_norm` to pure while improving scale check (#679)

* convert proc to pure, comment out scale check

* switch back rvs_norm to impure

zigset changes global parameters and thus can't be pure

* remove comments and prettify

* added protection against scale<=0

* fix nan implementation

* add ieee_value

* fprettify -i 4

---------

Procedures `pdf_norm` and `cdf_norm` of module `stdlib_stats_distribution_normal` are defined as `impure` because they include a call to the custom function `error_stop` if scale==0. This leads to the following problems/limitations:
1. Being impure, these functions can't be used in parallel applications.
1. There is no check if scale<0.
1. If, in a given parallel process, array evaluation. etc., a single function call is invalid the whole calculation process is stopped.

**Solution proposed**
1. Extend check scale==0 to scale<=0.
2. If scale <=0, return NaN rather than calling `error_stop(msg)`. Note: replacing `error_stop(msg)` by `error stop msg` would solve problem 1, but would not solve problem 3.
3.  Upgrade said functions to pure.

**Note:** The same approach can probably be used to other functions in this module, but I will wait for feedback before "expanding" the              
A tangential comment about icons: I noticed that whenever we add new icons we introduce new image files for the icons. Would it not be a better solution to rely on an icons library? Since we're using Bootstrap for UI elements, we could use Bootstrap Icons library for button icons. Nothing to do about this in this PR, just wanted to bring it up.

_Originally posted by @milancurcic in https://github.com/fortran-lang/playground/issues/63#issuecomment-1448717264_
